subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Completed high school diploma in the United States
Did not attend college or university after high school
Received on-the-job training at the auto auction facility
Attended company safety and compliance briefings during onboarding

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Works as a Transportation Planner at an auto auction company
Role is part of the CRC (Customer Receiving Center) department
Responsible for checking in vehicles, tagging them, and managing their movement to reconditioning
Acts as the first point of contact for incoming vehicle shipments

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Employed in the automotive remarketing and auction industry
Works within the transportation and logistics sector
Focuses on the movement, preparation, and sale of used vehicles

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Company has a mid-sized workforce, with multiple operational departments
Works on a large auto auction lot with both indoor and outdoor facilities
Team is part of a fast-paced, high-volume environment, especially before weekly auctions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Business specializes in wholesale auto auctions for dealerships and commercial buyers
Operates in the used vehicle remarketing segment
Handles vehicle intake, reconditioning, and preparation for weekly auctions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 1-2 years of experience in current transportation planning role
Joined the company in the last two years
Gained all relevant industry experience within this job tenure

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Holds an entry-level to junior position within the CRC department
Not in a managerial or supervisory role
Reports to a department supervisor or manager

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Checks in incoming vehicles by scanning VIN numbers using a handheld device
Tags vehicles and records their status in the company system
Marks vehicle windows with pencil crayon to indicate reconditioning instructions
Moves vehicles to the reconditioning center for cleaning and preparation
Handles paperwork to ensure cars are properly logged and accounted for

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends approximately 60% of the day scanning and checking in vehicles
Allocates around 20% of time to moving vehicles to the reconditioning center
Uses 10% of work time on documentation and paperwork related to vehicle intake
Devotes about 10% to communication with the detail shop and coordinating logistics

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Operates within a clearly defined process for checking in and tagging vehicles
Limited autonomy; follows established procedures and checklists
Can make minor decisions about vehicle flow and timing, but major issues are escalated
Relies on supervisor guidance for exceptions or unusual situations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Scanning VIN numbers and logging vehicles into the system is straightforward
Tagging cars and marking windows with instructions is done quickly and accurately
Physically moving cars to the designated area is routine and requires little thought

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Managing vehicle intake during high-volume periods is stressful
Dealing with discrepancies between paperwork and actual vehicle condition can be complex
Coordinating with multiple teams (e.g., detail shop, reconditioning) when vehicles have special requirements

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: VIN scanning and data entry is highly repetitive
Tagging and logging routine vehicle information is data-heavy and could be automated
Transcribing information from paperwork to digital systems is monotonous

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys tasks that involve moving and organizing cars on the lot
Finds repetitive scanning and paperwork to be tedious
Prefers hands-on work over administrative or data entry tasks
Engagement increases when working as part of a team during busy periods

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Occasional connectivity issues with handheld devices slow down intake
Paperwork errors or missing documentation cause delays
Limited automation leads to duplicated data entry
High volume of cars in short timeframes can create bottlenecks

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Feels more productive and satisfied when tasks are hands-on and active
Enjoyment decreases when performing repetitive or purely clerical tasks
Skill level is higher for physical and procedural tasks than for troubleshooting technology issues
Productivity peaks during well-coordinated team efforts

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Open to learning new technology if it improves workflow or efficiency
Mildly cautious; prefers hands-on demonstrations over manuals
Sees value in practical applications rather than theoretical tech knowledge

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts moderately well to new software with proper training
May require additional time or repetition to master unfamiliar systems
Can incorporate new methods if they are clearly presented and relevant to daily work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to attend short, focused training sessions during work hours
Less likely to pursue tech training on personal time
Prefers training that is directly related to current job responsibilities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by potential for time savings and reduced manual work
Heavy workload can be a barrier to participating in training
Skeptical of technology that seems unrelated to job tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: More likely to adopt new tools if peers and supervisors endorse them
Looks to coworkers for informal tips and troubleshooting advice
Management support increases confidence in new technology rollouts

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses a proprietary handheld device for VIN scanning and vehicle check-in
Accesses the company’s vehicle inventory management system multiple times per shift
Relies on Wi-Fi or mobile network connectivity for data syncing

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Utilizes pencil crayons for window marking and instruction
Uses paper logs and checklists for backup documentation
Regularly references printed intake paperwork attached to each vehicle

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Understands basic features of handheld inventory scanners
Familiar with digital systems for tracking and logging vehicle information
Limited experience with more advanced transportation planning software

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasional syncing delays between handheld device and main inventory system
Manual data entry required when paperwork does not match digital records
No direct integration between window markings and digital systems

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Has basic understanding of AI as software that can automate or assist with tasks
Recognizes terms like 'machine learning' and 'language models' from workplace discussions
Limited technical knowledge of how AI works under the hood

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Has occasionally used large language models (LLMs) for specific tasks at work
No formal AI platforms implemented at the auto auction company yet
Experience with LLMs likely through web interfaces or basic chatbots

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses LLMs on an occasional basis for ad hoc tasks, not daily
May have used LLMs for drafting emails or quick information lookups
No current AI workflow for core vehicle intake or logistics tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: Found LLM helpful for quickly summarizing procedures or generating checklists
LLM occasionally misunderstood industry-specific terminology, requiring manual correction
No critical errors, but noticed limitations in LLM's understanding of auction processes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: No formal organizational training on AI tools at this time
Relies on informal advice from coworkers familiar with LLM usage
Would benefit from structured training if AI tools are rolled out more widely

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers clear, step-by-step guidance when using AI tools
Values being able to work independently but appreciates AI support for complex tasks
Wants the option to override or adjust AI recommendations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI as an assistant that helps with repetitive or data-heavy tasks
Wants final decision-making authority to remain with human staff
Comfortable with AI suggesting next steps or flagging potential issues

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Open to adopting AI-driven workflows if they demonstrate clear efficiency gains
Would like to see pilot programs or phased rollouts before full adoption
Prefers gradual change over abrupt process overhauls

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Finds conversational interfaces user-friendly for quick information requests
Prefers command-based interfaces for structured, repetitive tasks
Would like the flexibility to choose interface style based on task complexity

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Some tasks, like identifying unusual vehicle conditions, require on-the-ground judgment
Basic intake and tagging are largely procedural and could be standardized
Special instructions for reconditioning may depend on tacit knowledge and experience

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI for routine data entry and checklist generation
Wants human review on tasks involving exceptions or unusual cases
Skeptical of AI recommendations without clear explanations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI to handle repetitive scanning and logging tasks
Believes humans should oversee quality control and exception handling
Sees value in AI augmenting, not replacing, human expertise

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for routine, low-risk tasks
Concerned about automation for tasks that require judgment or customer interaction
Wants assurance that jobs will not be eliminated solely due to automation

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI could reduce the need for manual data entry skills
Thinks soft skills like teamwork and communication will remain important
Sees potential for AI to enhance problem-solving skills by freeing up time

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: May need to learn how to troubleshoot or manage AI-assisted workflows
Could take on roles in training others on new systems
Might be responsible for interpreting and validating AI-generated outputs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Mildly concerned about automation of basic intake tasks
Does not feel immediate threat to job security, but is watchful of future changes
Believes core responsibilities will evolve rather than disappear

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Insists on having manual override options for all AI-supported processes
Believes human review should be mandatory for exceptions
Would be uncomfortable with fully automated decision-making in current context

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: No formal company policies on AI adoption yet
Anticipates that future policies will require transparency and training
Believes company will move cautiously and seek input from staff before major changes

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will increase efficiency in the automotive and logistics industries
Worries about potential job displacement in certain roles
Sees AI as a tool that can improve service quality if implemented thoughtfully

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Supports ethical guidelines for AI use to protect worker interests
Concerns about privacy and surveillance if AI tracks employee actions
Believes in transparency about how AI decisions are made

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Would like AI tools that automate check-in and tagging based on image recognition
Desires voice-activated interfaces for hands-free data entry
Wants AI-driven alerts for paperwork discrepancies or missing information

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects more automation of routine intake and logging tasks
Believes focus will shift to quality control and exception management
Anticipates new responsibilities in AI oversight and troubleshooting

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Wants early involvement of frontline staff in AI tool evaluation
Recommends phased pilot programs before full-scale rollout
Requests clear training and support resources for all employees
Prefers transparent communication about the goals and limitations of AI adoption
